text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 13 January 2019

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy; history of traumatic brain injury remote; past antiseizure medication side effects documented historically; mood symptoms under review; hypertension; dyslipidaemia; smoker.

Current medication: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily; Atorvastatin 20 mg once daily; Amlodipine 5 mg once daily; Omeprazole 20 mg once daily; Thiamine 100 mg once daily; Vitamin D3 1000 IU once daily.

I reviewed William Harris in clinic today, attending alone. He reports good general health and has returned to part‑time work in a warehouse, avoiding heavy lifting. Sleep is variable due to shift patterns. Appetite is stable and weight unchanged since the last visit.

Epilepsy: He has generalised epilepsy. He describes brief myoclonic twitches of the upper limbs predominantly in the mornings, without loss of awareness, and no recent convulsions. In line with his diary and his verbal account, the overall pattern has been of sporadic jerks this year. He associates these with late nights and missed breakfast. There have been no emergency presentations, injuries, or tongue bites. No known triggers other than sleep deprivation and caffeine excess. Driving is not undertaken.

Adverse effects: He denies current dizziness, diplopia, or mood lability on his present regimen. Past antiseizure medication side effects (e.g., rash or gastrointestinal upset) are documented historically in prior records; he has not experienced these recently. No issues with adherence.

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Power full in all limbs, coordination normal, gait steady. No tremor or asterixis. Vitals: BP 128/78, pulse 72 regular.

Investigations: Last routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs, vitamin D) three months ago were within reference ranges. No new imaging indicated. Prior EEG showed generalised spike‑wave discharges consistent with generalised epilepsy.

Plan:
- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily and sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily without change.
- Reinforce lifestyle measures: regular sleep, avoid excessive caffeine, and maintain hydration, as these appear to correlate with the sporadic jerks this year.
- Annual metabolic monitoring: arrange LFTs, FBC, and vitamin D in 9 months if not checked sooner in primary care.
- Safety advice reviewed; seizure first aid information provided.
- Follow‑up in 12 months, sooner if frequency increases, generalised tonic‑clonic seizures occur, or adverse effects emerge.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: the overall pattern has been of sporadic jerks this year

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes ongoing brief myoclonic twitches predominantly in the mornings, with no recent convulsions. It states the overall pattern has been of 'sporadic jerks this year' and links them to sleep deprivation and caffeine. There is no explicit numeric frequency (per day/week/month/year), no count per cluster, and no duration confirming seizure freedom for ≥6 months. Therefore, the exact frequency cannot be determined from the provided information.
seizure_frequency_number: the overall pattern has been of sporadic jerks this year
